rulings.Case Title: PREMIER FINANCIAL SERVICES LLC, A CONNECTICUT VS. ADI SIMAYOF et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Pursuant To Ccp 437c, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF PREMIER FINANCIAL SERVICES LLC'S Motion For Summary Judgment Pursuant To CCP Sec. 437c, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Plaintiff Premier Financial Services LLC' motion for summary judgment and in the alternative summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Based on the declaration of defendant Adi Simayof, there are numerous triable disputes which preclude the granting of any portion of this motion. The most notable triable dispute is whether Mr. Siamyof is obligated on the lease which is the subject of all four of Premier's claims. Premier's arguments that this court should disregard Mr. Simayof's declaration run afoul of the most basic rule on summary judgment: the court is not permitted to evaluate credibility or otherwise weigh the evidence, but must only identify whether there is a triable dispute, however improbable it may be. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Simayof is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: SEIU-USWW, AN UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATION ET AL VS. PREFERRED BUILDING SERVICES, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Class Certification
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 4, PLAINTIFF JUAN MARTINEZ'S Motion For Class Certification. Plaintiff Juan Martinez's motion for class certification is granted. The following class is certified: thirty janitors and handymen, who had been employed by VPM Maintenance Management, LLC for more than eight months as of April 10, 2015 when their employment with VPM ended and who were not subsequently retained by defendant Preferred Buildings Services, Inc. Mr. Martinez's request to act as a class representative is granted. Mr. Martinez has shown that the class is ascertainable. The class is sufficiently numerous. A common issue of law - whether Preferred violated the law by not retaining Mr. Martinez and the other members of the class - predominates and is based on a set of facts that is common to all class members. The possibility that one of class members may not be eligible to receive damages or that the determination of the amount of damages will be based on individual assessments does not show that individual issues predominate or otherwise preclude certification. (Sav-on Drug Stores, Inc. v. Superior Court (2004) 34 Cal.4th 319, 333 ("a class action is not inappropriate simply because each member of the class may at some point be required to make an individual showing as to his or her eligibility for recovery or as to the amount of his or her damages.")). There is no evidence that Preferred's waiver defense requires individualized determinations of fact as to each class member's intent when signing a severance agreement because the Union that negotiated all the agreements, explained the agreements to the workers, the agreements' terms were identical and the contracting party to all of the agreements states that it did not bargain for a waiver of statutory rights. Mr. Martinez has shown that his claim is typical of the class and he can adequately represent the class. While Preferred's theory of the case is that Mr. Martinez is not entitled to recover because any damages resulting from Preferred's conduct has been offset by the amount of Mr. Martinez's severance package, there is no showing that this theory is "a defense unique to the class representative [and] will be a major focus of the litigation" or that it renders Mr. Martinez's interests "antagonistic to or in conflict with the objectives of those ¿ he purports to represent." (Medrazo v. Honda of North Hollywood (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 89, 99). Class treatment serves judicial economy by avoiding litigation of similar issues as well as benefits the class by enabling workers, less able to litigate individually due to linguistic barriers and limited comprehension of the legal issues, to obtain recovery. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Martinez is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ZHONGYING DING VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Francisco Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication. (Tentative Ruling Part 1 of 2) Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first cause of action for dangerous condition of public property and granted as to the second cause of action for failure to warn of a dangerous condition of public property. There is a triable dispute whether the planters constitute a dangerous condition of public property. CCSF essentially argues that the planters are not a dangerous condition because one exercising due care would recognize the customary use of the planters and not climb on them. CCSF misstates the law. "[A] dangerous condition may exist when the physical condition of the premises is viewed in conjunction with its actual, intended, or reasonably anticipated use." (Bartell v. Palos Verdes Peninsula School Dist. (1978) 83 Cal.App.3d 492, 496-497 (emphasis added); see also Torkelson v. City of Redlands (1961) 198 Cal.App.2d 354, 361 (unfenced storm drain played in by children was a dangerous condition of public property).) There is evidence that it was reasonably anticipated that persons would climb on the planters and CCSF acknowledges that climbing on the planters has been a problem for years. (UMF 36-40.) Whether plaintiff Zhonying Ding was comparatively at fault is a distinct issue from the issue of whether the planters constitute a dangerous condition of public property. There is also a triable dispute whether Ms. Ding was engaged in a "hazardous recreational activity" within the meaning of Government Code 831.7. Ms. Ding testified that she was sightseeing and taking photos. (Ding Depo. 35:24-36:16.) There is another triable dispute whether design immunity per Government Code 830.6 applies to this case. "By¿its very terms the design immunity of section 830.6 is limited to a design-caused accident. It does not immunize from liability caused by negligence independent of design, even though the independent negligence is only a concurring, proximate cause of the accident." (Flournoy v. State (1969) 275 Cal.App.2d 806, 811)(citation omitted)). (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ZHONGYING DING VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 5, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco's Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Entry Of Tentative Ruling). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) In this case, the injury was not caused by the design of the planters. Rather, it occurred because the planters were no longer used as planters and they were used as stairs. There is also a disputed issue whether design immunity applies because the physical conditions of the planters changed. "We hold that where a plan or design of a construction of, or improvement to, public property, although shown to have been reasonably approved in advance or prepared in conformity with standards previously so approved, as being safe, nevertheless in its actual operation under changed physical conditions produces a dangerous condition of public property and causes injury, the public entity does not retain the statutory immunity from liability conferred on it by section 830.6." (Baldwin v. State of California (1972) 6 Cal.3d 424, 438). There is evidence on this motion that the planters no longer had vegetation or trees to dissuade climbing on them. Ms. Ding's second cause of action refers to Government Code 830.8, but that statute is inapposite as it refers to warning signs "described in the Vehicle Code." The second cause of action also refers to Government Code 835.2(b). That subsection relates to the constructive notice element of Ms. Ding's first cause of action and her remedy is to pursue that claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Ding is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 6, Plaintiff Paige Barham's Motion To Compel Production Of Documents, Privilege Log, Interrogatory Responses, Request For Admission Response, And For Sanctions. Plaintiff Paige Barham's motion to compel production of documents, privilege log, interrogatory responses, request for admission response, and for sanctions is continued to June 7, 2018 to give the parties an opportunity to engage in meaningful face to face meet and confer in an effort to resolve all of their disagreements about the discovery that is the subject of this motion. If any discovery disputes remain after the meeting, by 4pm on June 6, 2018 counsel for Ms. Barham must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to counsel for defendants identifying, without argument, each discovery request that remains in dispute. For guidance of the parties in their meeting, the court: 1) expects that the parties will make good faith efforts to agree on the language of a protective order; 2) believes that all responsive documents that are withheld on the grounds of privilege that are dated prior to the service of the complaint on defendants need to be identified in a privilege log; 3) admonishes defendants that a great majority of the discovery sought by Ms. Barham is permissible and that further responses by defendants should include all information within the possession, custody and control of defendants as of the date of the responses; and 4) advises Ms. Barham that the personnel file and other personal information of the driver need not be provided. rulings.Case Title: GIANNA GALLETTA VS. FCA US, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Vehicle Inspection And Request For Monetary Sanctions [Ccp 2031.310, 2023.010]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT FCA US, LLC, PUTNAM CHRYSLER JEEP DODGE's Motion To Compel Vehicle Inspection And Request For Monetary Sanctions [Ccp 2031.310, 2023.010] Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Before the Court are Defendants' Motion to Compel A Vehicle Inspection of the 2014 Jeep Patriot which is the subject of this action and for $5262.50 in sanctions and Plaintiff's motion for a protective order seeking to preclude Defendants from conducting a "second" vehicle inspection of the Jeep Patriot. Both motions arise from an effort by Defendants to inspect the vehicle on April 27, 2018. The Court GRANTS the motion to compel and orders the parties to meet and confer to select a date for an inspection to occur within the next 20 days at a place of Defendants' selection. The Court further orders that the protocol laid out in paragraphs 2 and 4-12 of the Court's order in Moshari v. Mercedes Benz (Nassirian Dec., Exh. Q) will govern the conduct of the inspection. Any data acquired from the inspection shall be available for production pursuant to the provisions of CCP section 2034.210 et seq. or upon the deposition of Defendants' expert. Defendants' motion for sanctions is DENIED for failure to include a "declaration setting forth facts supporting the amount of any monetary sanction sought." CCP section 2023.040. rulings.Case Title: AMRITPAL SINGH VS. MICHAEL MORGAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 16, DEFENDANT MICHAEL MORGAN'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Michael Morgan's demurrer to the third, fourth, seventh, and eighth causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Amritpal Singh is: a) sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for false imprisonment and the eighth cause of action for gross negligence and b) overruled as to the fourth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress and the seventh cause of action for conspiracy. The face of the complaint shows that the false imprisonment claim is time-barred. Liberally construed the complaint alleges sufficient facts to state an IIED and liability based on a conspiracy theory. Demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and strictly construed because discovery can provide clarification. (Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135). "California does not recognize a distinct cause of action for 'gross negligence' independent of a statutory basis." (Eriksson v. Nunnink (2011) 191 Cal.App.4th 826, 856, fn. 18). There is no need for Mr. Singh to allege gross negligence within the negligence claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Morgan is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MARC BORACCHIA ET AL VS. RYAN FISTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 11, DEFENDANTS RYAN FISTER AND SUNSHINE FISTER'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendants Ryan and Sunshine Fister's anti-SLAPP motion to strike both causes of action alleged in the complaint filed by plaintiffs Marc Boracchia and Lea Wiviott-Boracchia is denied as to both causes of action and plaintiffs' request for fees is also denied. Assuming without deciding that plaintiffs' claims arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute, plaintiffs have satisfied their second prong burden of showing that there is minimal merit to each of their claims. Per the reverse summary judgment standard used to evaluate whether the second prong burden has been met, plaintiffs have shown that there is evidence which a reasonable trier of fact could find that one or both of the allegedly false statements attributed to defendants were provably false and that defendants made those statements with malice which defeats the qualified common interest privilege. Moreover, plaintiffs met their second prong burden that their claims are not barred by the litigation privilege because the allegedly false statements attributed to defendants were not made preliminary to or during a judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding. Because plaintiffs have not shown this motion is frivolous, their fees request is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JAMES SPINGOLA VS. KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 13, CROSS-DEFENDANT KAISER FOUNDATION HEALTH PLAN, INC.'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINT. Cross-defendant Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc.'s demurrer to all three causes of action in the cross-complaint of Aguatierra Associates, Inc. is overruled in its entirety. Kaiser's argument that the equitable indemnity claim is premature is unpersuasive. Even though Weiss has yet to pay any funds to Mr. Spingola, Weiss can bring a declaratory relief cause of action for equitable indemnity in this underlying tort action. (E.L. White, Inc. v. City of Huntington Beach, (1978) 21 Cal. 3d 497, 506; People ex rel. Dept. of Transportation v. Sup. Crt. (1980) 26 Cal.3d 744, 759-760; Valley Circle Estates v. VTN Consolidated, Inc. (1983) 33 Cal.3d 606, 612.) Kaiser's distinction between the equitable indemnity cause of action and the declaratory relief cause of action has little or no practical effect and both should proceed for the sake of judicial efficiency. This same reasoning applies to the contribution cause of action. The scope of the indemnification provision and whether its terms displace Weiss' equitable indemnity rights are not amenable to resolution on a demurrer. Paragraph 11.1 [indemnification] of the agreement, which the parties agree may be judicially noticed, refers to indemnity with respect to the asbestos work. Mr. Spingola's amended complaint and the cross-complaint do not allege claims involving asbestos work and extrinsic evidence is necessary to resolve whether that reference in the contract is a typographical error. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Weiss is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ANTHONY BERRY VS. CHRISTINA CARTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Anthony Berry To Answer Questions At Deposition And To Produce For Inspection Documents Described In Deposition Notice
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, May 31, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT CHRISTINA CARTER, MARK HOGENSON's Motion To Compel Anthony Berry To Answer Questions At Deposition And To Produce For Inspection Documents Described In Deposition Notice. Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)
